The Alpha Project

The Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation

The Molecular Sciences Institute Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation (CGEC) was created to pursue interdisciplinary projects that will enable broad understanding of cellular and organismic behavior and work towards making predictive models of biological systems. This understanding will serve to radically improve researchers' abilities to understand biology by providing them with advanced methods and tools to probe important biological questions. The National Human Genome Research Institute (NHGRI) of the National Institutes of Health designated the CGEC as a Center of Excellence in Genomic Sciences in July 2002. This award is continued acknowledgement of the MSI's past and future research contributions to the creation of this new field of predictive biology.

The flagship activity at the CGEC is the Alpha Project. The focus of the Alpha Project is to examine extra- to intra-cellular information flow and processing, (how cells receive, amplify, and integrate signals from a variety of stimuli). Signal transduction is an integral part of most cellular processes. Quantifying how cells sense and respond to various stimuli and creating a model that will accurately predict these responses are the first important steps to understanding diseases and eventually to tailoring precise treatments to each individual. Gaining this level of understanding will require the development of new research methods and computational tools to probe and analyze such complex biological processes. The Alpha Project is an intensive five-year, multidisciplinary research effort to work towards this increased level of understanding. Joining the MSI researchers in this effort are collaborating CGEC members from the California Institute of Technology, the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, the University of California, Berkeley, and Pacific Northwest National Laboratory.

People
Roger Brent President and Research Director
Shuki Bruck Modeling, biological information theory (Caltech)
Ian Burbulis Biochemistry and proteomics
Tina Chin Experimental methods development
Alejandro Colman-Lerner Experimental and computational biology
Drew Endy Modeling, methods development, experimental integration (MIT)
Andrew Gordon Modeling and methods development
Lauren Ha Administration
Julie Leary Mass spectrometry, methods development (UC Berkeley)
Larry Lok Biological modeling; simulation methods development
Orna Resnekov Modeling, methods development, mass spectrometry
Richard Smith Mass spectrometry, methods development (PNL)
David Soergel Bioinformatics databases and computation
Jeremy Thorner Yeast genetics (UC Berkeley)
Dawn Warfield Assistant to the President
Richard Yu Modeling, single cell measurement methods, peptide aptamer development, structural biology

Subproject of: Research@MSI
